summary: The paper investigates the internal mechanics of large vision-language model (LVLM) decoders through an information-theoretic and geometric lens. Grounded in the manifold hypothesis, it introduces two complementary metrics: Representation Information Discrepancy (RID), which quantifies the semantic innovation a module injects by expanding the representation's spectrum and support, and Mixing Information Gain (MixIG), which quantifies how a module reconfigures or redistributes existing information within the representation manifold. Applying these metrics across a large set of LVLM families (around fifteen), the paper reports a consistent functional decoupling: FFN modules behave as subspace-expanding operators that drive semantic innovation, whereas attention modules behave as subspace-preserving operators that mainly reconfigure existing information. Building on the observation that attention contributes little innovation in many layers, the authors run intervention experiments (Shared Attention Prior, SAP) in which learned visual attention weights are replaced by fixed non-parametric priors or even Gaussian noise, and find that this often preserves or even improves benchmark accuracy (e.g., on POPE, MMMU, and MathVista). They interpret this as evidence of substantial redundancy and misallocation in current LVLM attention, arguing that state-of-the-art models effectively "get lost in attention" rather than efficiently exploiting visual context.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper offers a technically grounded and clearly motivated framework for dissecting the residual-stream updates of LVLM decoders. Grounding the analysis in the manifold hypothesis and information theory, and separating each module's contribution into semantic innovation (RID) and information reconfiguration (MixIG), yields a clean and interpretable vocabulary for the roles of attention and FFN modules, and represents a genuine step beyond purely attribution-based analysis. I appreciated that the metrics are not merely asserted but validated, both through stochastic (noise) baselines and through consistency across roughly fifteen LVLM families, which lends credibility and generality to the central claim of a functional decoupling in which FFNs drive innovation while attention largely redistributes existing information. The Shared Attention Prior (SAP) intervention is the most compelling part of the paper: replacing learned visual attention with fixed priors or even Gaussian noise while preserving, and occasionally improving, accuracy on benchmarks such as POPE, MMMU, and MathVista is a simple and direct demonstration of the claimed redundancy, and it is likely to provoke useful discussion. The framing is timely, and the paper is on the whole well organized and clearly written.

That said, several aspects of the empirical argument leave me with reservations. My main soundness concern is how the layers (and heads) for the SAP/noise interventions are chosen: the selected ranges vary across models and appear to be set manually rather than derived from the proposed metrics. This raises the possibility that the reported results reflect post-hoc, per-model configurations rather than a universal phenomenon, and it somewhat undercuts the paper's strongest claim. A principled, automated, metric-driven criterion for identifying redundant layers, ideally one that transfers to an unseen architecture, would substantially strengthen the work and turn an interesting observation into an actionable tool. Relatedly, the paper documents the decoupling and the redundancy but offers little causal explanation for either: why substituting learned attention with noise sustains or improves performance, and why the functional split between attention and FFN arises (e.g., limitations of the visual encoder, architectural constraints, or the training objective), are left largely open.

A second, more technical concern is the alignment between the theoretical framing and the interventions. In the method, the representation X appears to denote the full token sequence (vision and language), whereas the SAP experiments replace attention only among vision tokens; this mismatch makes it somewhat unclear what the metrics are actually measured over and how precisely the intervention maps onto the analysis. Given the trend toward unified multimodal processing, an explicit treatment of cross-modal interactions, distinguishing visual-visual self-attention redundancy from visual-text cross-modal redundancy and identifying which one drives the effect, would both clarify the mechanism and broaden the contribution.

On significance, while the redundancy result is provocative, the observation that attention is not strictly necessary is not entirely new (it echoes findings such as MetaFormer in the vision-transformer setting), so clearer positioning relative to prior interpretability and architecture work would help establish what is genuinely new beyond a more fine-grained, LVLM-specific analysis. The study is also predominantly post-hoc and inference-time; it is not yet shown whether the insights can guide training (for example, pretraining with fixed SAP layers to save compute), which is where much of the potential impact lies. Finally, the practicality of the diagnostics deserves attention: RID and MixIG rely on SVD and entropy estimation over high-dimensional hidden states, and the paper does not discuss their computational cost or scalability for long-context LVLMs. Despite these concerns, the information-theoretic and geometric framing is a fresh and useful diagnostic perspective on LVLM internals, and the intervention findings are a valuable empirical contribution.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can RID and MixIG be used to derive a principled, automated criterion for identifying redundant layers/heads for SAP on an unseen model, rather than manual per-model selection? Would the SAP results remain robust under a uniform selection rule (e.g., a fixed top-X% of layers with consistent head-selection criteria) applied across all models?
2. Is the observed attention redundancy primarily a consequence of the training objective (models eventually learning to rely on FFNs for innovation) or an inherent architectural bottleneck of attention over high-dimensional visual tokens? Do you have any preliminary evidence from training models with fixed SAP layers from scratch?
3. In the method, does X denote the full token sequence or only vision tokens, and how does this align with SAP replacing only vision-token attention? Does the analysis distinguish visual-visual self-attention redundancy from visual-text cross-modal redundancy, and which of these primarily drives the "lost in attention" phenomenon?
4. What is the computational overhead of RID and MixIG (SVD plus entropy estimation on high-dimensional hidden states) for long-context LVLMs, and can the metrics be efficiently approximated for practical, real-time diagnostics?
5. Performance appears more sensitive to the intervention on OCR and document/chart understanding tasks; for which tasks is attention genuinely redundant versus necessary, and what is the rationale for attention becoming more critical in these fine-grained reasoning settings?
limitations: The limitations discussion should be strengthened. In particular, the paper should explicitly scope its central claim by acknowledging that the observed "attention redundancy" may be specific to current LVLM architectures and general-purpose benchmarks, and discuss whether it persists in fine-grained or specialized visual domains (e.g., medical imaging, high-resolution analysis) and for cross-modal vision-text fusion. The computational cost and scalability of the RID/MixIG diagnostics for long-context settings should also be stated as a practical limitation.
